name: pnpm
context: pnpm package manager ecosystem for deterministic installs, monorepos, filtered execution, and safer dependency workflows
info: pnpm is a Node.js package manager built around a content-addressable global store, a virtual `node_modules/.pnpm` layout, and first-class workspace support. It matters to autonomous agents because it changes how dependencies are installed, how commands are scoped in monorepos, how deployable subsets are produced, and how build-script trust is enforced. This ecosystem focuses on the jobs agents perform repeatedly: lockfile-driven installs, package mutations in one workspace, recursive script execution, deployment packaging, patching upstream packages, and configuring pnpm-specific safety and runtime controls.
body:
# pnpm

pnpm is a fast package manager built around a reusable global store and a virtual dependency graph under `node_modules/.pnpm`. For an agent, the important part is not just speed: pnpm changes resolution, workspace targeting, deployment packaging, and supply-chain controls.

## Agent jobs

- Install deterministically from `pnpm-lock.yaml` with [[pnpm/install]]
- Scope monorepo work with [[pnpm/workspace#filters]] and the `workspace:` protocol
- Run local binaries and scripts with [[pnpm/scripts]]
- Produce a portable output directory with [[pnpm/deploy]]
- Patch broken upstream packages with [[pnpm/patch]]

## Key files

```yaml
# pnpm-workspace.yaml
packages:
  - apps/*
  - packages/*
```

```json
{
  "packageManager": "pnpm@10.23.0"
}
```

Use Corepack or another official installer to pin the package-manager version, then keep `pnpm-lock.yaml` committed. In automation, prefer `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` and cache the pnpm store instead of treating `node_modules` as the primary cache.

sections.title: Install and version pinning
sections.body: Official installation docs recommend Node.js 18.12+ when not using the standalone installer, and Corepack can pin the project version by writing a `packageManager` field. Use `corepack enable pnpm` and `corepack use pnpm@latest-10` when you need reproducible team-wide pnpm versions. Once pnpm is present, [[pnpm/install]] covers lockfile-driven installs and dependency changes.

sections.title: Workspace model
sections.body: A pnpm workspace is rooted by `pnpm-workspace.yaml`. Local packages should usually depend on each other with the `workspace:` protocol so pnpm refuses registry fallback. Filters such as `foo...`, `...foo`, `{packages/**}`, and `[origin/main]` let an agent narrow changes to only the relevant part of a monorepo. See [[pnpm/workspace]] for the execution patterns and graph selectors.

sections.title: Supply-chain controls
sections.body: pnpm has explicit controls for dependency scripts and release trust. Recent versions add `approve-builds`, `allowBuilds`, `minimumReleaseAge`, trust policies, and patch workflows. These controls matter when an agent installs unreviewed third-party code or needs to unblock a broken dependency without dropping all safeguards. See [[pnpm/security]] and [[pnpm/patch]].

npm solves the same class of package-management jobs but with different workspace and store behavior
